evidence_excerpt: After the first specialist evaluation at age 6, subsequent testing showed worsening peripheral neuropathy: EMG at 7 years had a discrete reduction in sural SNAP amplitude, and by 9 years EMG was consistent with sensitive axonal polyneuropathy. The report also notes a typical AOA1 course of slowly progressive cerebellar ataxia.

quality_of_life.evidence_excerpt: Persistent neurological signs after index time included ataxic gait, oculomotor apraxia, distal limb dystonia, and EMG-confirmed axonal polyneuropathy, indicating ongoing prominent symptoms affecting daily life.

timeline_events.evidence_excerpt: At age 7, low vitamin E levels were treated with oral vitamin E; levels returned to normal thereafter.
By age 9, EMG findings progressed to sensitive axonal polyneuropathy, indicating objective neurological worsening compared to earlier testing.

notes_on_uncertainty: The case report provides limited follow-up details after definitive APTX deletion diagnosis; thus the first specialist evaluation at age 6 was used as the index time. Functional and symptom assessments are inferred from clinician descriptions, and long-term outcomes are based on EMG progression and the stated slowly progressive nature of AOA1. No explicit post-diagnosis functional improvement or hospital readmissions were documented.
